critique.summary: Reviewed README.md, schema.py, and serializer.py. The package has multiple concrete contract and correctness defects in serializer.py: required fields are silently defaulted, one field is emitted with the wrong type, and validation is both incomplete and only applied to the first record.

findings.description: serializer.py::to_record silently injects version=1 when the input omits "version" (data.get("version", 1)). README.md and schema.py both say version is required and has no default, so a missing version must raise an error instead of being fabricated.

serializer.py::to_record silently injects items=[] when the input omits "items" (data.get("items", [])). items is marked required in schema.FIELDS, so this turns a missing required field into a valid-looking record instead of reporting an error.

serializer.py::to_record stores count as str(n) instead of an int. schema.py declares count.type == "int", and README.md says count is an INTEGER equal to len(items), so the function currently emits schema-invalid records such as count="3".

serializer.py::to_record does not enforce the declared type of version. If the caller passes {"version": "1", "items": []}, the function returns version="1" unchanged even though schema.FIELDS requires an int, so to_record can return records that do not match the schema it claims to produce.

serializer.py::validate only inspects records[0]. Any invalid record after the first one is ignored, so validate([valid_record, invalid_record]) can incorrectly return True. This directly contradicts README.md, which says validate(records) validates every record, not just the first.

serializer.py::validate checks only that required field names are present; it never verifies the schema-declared field types. As a result, records like {"version": "x", "count": "2", "items": []} pass validation even though schema.py requires version and count to be ints.
